item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite funny skips. +1
cool and good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has got to be are we cool yet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are you talking about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the only SCPs that made me laugh out loud. It was the wallet strapped to a brick line. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you possibly cover up a yacht dropping out of the sky with that much water?? +1 anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't noticed how passive-aggressive the prizes were until reading this comment, and now I can't stop chuckling. This is a great example of a well written, non-world-ending Euclid. Kudos!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I had to chew on it for a few hours, because it's quite understated, but this is fantastic.

My favorite part is how passive-aggressive the "prizes" are, almost like they didn't expect anyone to win. "Oh, crap, you won…? Umm, here's a wallet I found I guess? Oh, you won a cruise, hotshot? Well eat this."

It makes me wonder what the motivation for these contests could possibly be, which is a mystery I like.

EDIT: Fixed a typo and removed some gratuitous profanity. Sorry, my enthusiasm gets the better of me sometimes!

item.title: first stage?
item.content:encoded:
"under observation by Foundation personnel for the first stage of SCP-1456"

First stage? of what? Does the foundation know of subsequent stages of this phenomenon?

item.title: I want to win!!!!
item.content:encoded: I want to win the "Computer and Desk" prize!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, I like it. I like the car crash, and the stolen wallet as "prizes". Maybe another prize could be some sort of maid, only to result in some sort of zombie-like thing? Anyways, I like it. You have my upvote, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM9FeEgI0Eo simply put, the dude died +1
And that, kids, is what we in the biz call an ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. Researcher walks outside of his home in the morning. Everything is calm. Suddenly, a shadow. Looks up to see 900 kilolitres of seawater, a boat, a whale, and a hundred fish floating above his house.

"Huh… That's not supposed to be-"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this concept could have been pushed a bit further, but I like it. The last two logs are definitely what give it its appeal. I'm not entirely sure I like the tone of the line "by methods both mundane and anomalous" though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was there a point to this comment…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sort of necro, but it's the same reason I upvoted this article. Though 900 kiloliters of water… if that much water poured in an instant the water might as well be a non-Newtonian fluid. It would have crushed the house and the poor agent, along with effectively being a water-based nuke. (1 mL = 1 gram of water; 1 L = 1 kilogram of water; 900 thousand kilograms of anything = 9 million Newtons of force… plus the fact that it's falling… I'm pretty sure xkcd had something to say about it, but it's roughly equivalent (according to Wikipedia) to dropping four blue whales on the house. http://what-if.xkcd.com/12/ But this is way more than what the water provided in this article was.

Now I realize that I don't really scrutinize the works I read, rather I get the idea and critique that. Might be a problem for me in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instance 1121 just made me laugh for about 10 seconds when I read the "Approximately 900 kilolitres of water" line, because that just spontaneously appearing (And probably drowning whoever was inside the house) looked so funny in my mind.

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this is a game to you, press 1.
If this is not a game to you, press 2.
To speak with a representative, press 37.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to upvote this comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting. With that said, I'd like more. This is a decent skeleton, but the flesh is a little sparse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly this is slightly true. This was the concept that originally evolved into the Doorstep Babies, when I thought maybe one of the things delivered should be a baby, and then I realized that weird babies on your doorstep is more interesting than a plague of contests. I was poking around my documents, came across this, and decided, what the hell, I'll clean it up and submit it, see what happens, and it just happens to be doing quite well for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Figuring out containment, and playing with or subverting the formula, is probably my favourite part of the SCP format. Every single SCP I've submitted has had containment protocols outside the norm, being something we can't nessesarily just keep in a box. They haven't figured out where the Doorstep Babies come from. The Ball and Chain can't be contained normally because it's implied to be a device for containing something else. These things arrive without source via a medium that cannot be stopped.

Amoung my SCPs that didn't make it, there was a set of instruments that prevented you from locking them away if you were aware the set was incomplete, an object that created mobile SCPs out in the world that then chased down individuals, and an instance of the Foundation containing the wrong thing, which I really have to revisit. There was a randomly located but consistent manifestation the Foundation had to do information control on, and a guy who had to write his own containment report because nobody else could.

The interesting part of the Foundation is not nessesarily the things they keep. It's the fact that they keep them and the methods they go through in order to do so. No matter how strange, frightening or powerful the object, if it is something that you can just keep in a box, even a very reinforced box, then it starts to lose my interest. Because anything interesting inside a box will probably be much more interesting if it gets out.

173 got that right straight off the bat. We can't just keep it in; some poor schmucks have to go clean out it's cage every once in a while. Most of my favourites do this too. 1377's source hasn't been located. They haven't found all of 1434. Copies of 1883 are still out there. 650 has to be watched at all times. You have to keep eating 871.

I like SCPs that escape, that have to be actively wrangled, that must be negotiated with, that are too big or too widespread or too engrained to contain. Things we have to hunt down, things originating from an unknown source, sets of things we haven't found all of yet. Things whose properties defy containment.

Because honestly, if we can just keep it in a box, it's not that scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last instance… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am also pretty neutral about this one. It's solid, and I like how you did the containment procedures, but it doesn't really grab me.

… You know what? I really like those containment procedures. Containing something this public is tricky to do well, but you did. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this a game to you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He felt that the author phoned it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost 2 days later, I'm still neutral about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was fairly neutral about this until the last two logs. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking twisted. (see rating history for conclusion)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It didn't ring his bell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a joke to be made here, but I can't for the life of me figure out what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This neither impressed me or intrigued me. This is quite dull for a phone-in SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was neutral, but on that last line, I was "Okay, fuck it, this gets an upvote."

That is how you do humor in SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"escalating until with no known upper limit until the phone is disconnected"
"in a blank envelop."
Just a couple of mistakes I noticed on my first read through. Will check again when I'm less sleepy if someone hasn't already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know I won't get your vote, but I've edited these sections.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The media and advertisements in the city of ██████, Colorado is

This needs some fixing in terms of verb-subject agreement.

vary immensely

Tone.

This doesn't really do anything for me. The wording is awkward in some way I just can't place my finger on, and it seems pretty boring to me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They really don't know. The only consistent mechanism is the call-in portion, as both the advertisement and the prizes vary both in content, quality, and level of strangeness involved in their delivery.

Retrospectively, I like to imagine that this is the equivalent of a series of prank calls being performed by a pantheon of minor deities, making up these fake contests for giggles and delivering prizes to the best of their various supernatural abilities. Alternately, this is a sort of primal force representing some kind of exchanged repurposed or repacked into call-in contests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The car crash example was what made me upvote. It's a great example of "Did the SCP cause the problem, or is it just taking advantage of it?"

One thing that concerns me: How does the Foundation know that these are all the same phenomena? The delivery methods are different enough that it seems like it could be several different anomalous sources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've won!

an upvote. I like it.
